followups.a: A template fixes the static skeleton as a byte-identical prefix and confines all per-request variance to clearly-bounded slots, so the cacheable portion stays stable while only the variable tail changes. Ad-hoc f-stringing tends to interleave dynamic values into the static text, perturbing the prefix and busting the cache.

q: For a long-running agent that reuses a large static prompt across many steps, how do you use Anthropic's 1-hour cache TTL effectively?
a: The default cache TTL is 5 minutes, but agent steps can exceed that gap, so Anthropic offers a 1-hour TTL that keeps the static prefix warm across slow multi-step runs and shared user sessions. Writes cost more (about 2x base for the 1-hour, 1.25x for the 5-minute) while reads are roughly 0.1x, so the 1-hour pays off only when the same prefix is reused many times within the hour. Caching covers tools, system, then messages in order up to your cache_control breakpoint, so put all static content first and dynamic turns after. Watch the 20-content-block-before-breakpoint caveat, use intermediate breakpoints for very large prefixes, and monitor read versus write token counts to confirm the cache is actually hitting.
fs: Knows the 1-hour TTL trades a higher write cost for surviving slow agent gaps, and that static-first ordering plus breakpoint placement drive hit rate.
fj: Enabling the 1-hour TTL everywhere and paying the 2x write premium even when the prefix is not reused enough to recoup it.

followups.q: When does the 1-hour TTL's 2x write premium fail to pay off?
followups.a: When the prefix isn't reused enough within the hour to amortize the higher write cost—a low-traffic feature, a one-shot call, or a session that ends quickly. Each read is ~0.1x, so you need enough hits to recoup the ~2x write; below that break-even the cheaper 5-minute TTL (1.25x write) or no extended cache wins. Compute expected reuse per hour before flipping it on globally.

followups.q: An agent appends a growing conversation history each step. How does that affect the cache?
followups.a: Each appended turn extends the prefix, so you want the static system/tools block cached behind a breakpoint and the growing messages after it; on each step the prior cached prefix is reused and only the new suffix is written. Place an incremental cache breakpoint after the latest stable turn so accumulated history also caches forward.

followups.q: What's the 20-content-block-before-breakpoint caveat about?
followups.a: Anthropic caches in segments and there are limits on cache breakpoints and how blocks group before one, so a very large prefix split awkwardly can leave part of it uncached or force extra writes. For big static contexts you place intermediate breakpoints to ensure each chunk caches cleanly rather than overflowing a segment.

q: What is assistant-prefill (priming the start of the model's reply), and what production problems does it solve?
a: Prefill seeds the beginning of the assistant turn so the model continues from text you supply rather than starting fresh, which constrains format and skips preamble. Starting the reply with an open brace forces it straight into JSON with no markdown fence or 'Sure, here is' chatter, and prefilling a chosen label or 'The answer is' steers a terse, parseable completion. It also reinforces a persona or stops the model from re-explaining the task every turn. The trade-offs are provider support varies and is limited or unavailable for some reasoning models, the prefill counts as output you must account for, and over-constraining can cut off legitimate reasoning, so use it for format control rather than to suppress needed thinking.
fs: Explains prefill continues the assistant turn to lock format and kill preamble, while noting reasoning-model limits and the over-constraint risk.
fj: Thinking prefill is just another instruction line, not actual seeded assistant tokens, or using it to suppress reasoning the task needs.

followups.q: You prefilled '{' to force JSON. What subtle parsing bug does that introduce?
followups.a: The model continues from your `{` but doesn't re-emit it, so the returned content starts after the brace—you must prepend the prefill text yourself before parsing, or `json.loads` fails on a missing opening brace. Forgetting that the prefill is part of the object but not in the response is the classic off-by-one. Reconstruct the full string as prefill + completion before validation.

followups.q: Why is prefill limited or risky on reasoning models?
followups.a: Reasoning models do internal thinking before the visible answer, and seeding the assistant turn can interfere with or truncate that reasoning phase—some providers disallow prefill with extended thinking for exactly this reason. Forcing the reply to start at a fixed token can cut off the chain the model needs.

followups.q: How does prefill compare to strict json_schema mode for forcing structure?
followups.a: json_schema mode is a hard decoder-level guarantee of shape across the whole object; prefill is a soft nudge that only constrains the opening, so the model can still drift into invalid JSON after the seed. Prefill is lighter, provider-portable, and good for killing preamble or steering a label, but for a real structural guarantee use constrained generation.

q: Why do negatively framed instructions like 'do not mention pricing' often fail, and how should you phrase constraints instead?
a: Negations are weaker steering signals because the model still attends to the forbidden concept you just named, and a long list of 'don'ts' is easy to partially miss. Positive, directive phrasing that says what to do instead, like 'redirect any pricing question to the sales team', gives the model a concrete behavior to execute rather than an absence to maintain. Where a rule is truly hard, enforce it outside the prompt with an output filter rather than hoping the wording holds. Keep constraints few, specific, and testable, since piling on prohibitions dilutes all of them.
fs: Explains why negation is a weak signal and reframes constraints as positive directives backed by an output filter for hard rules.
fj: Stacking many 'do not' rules and assuming the model reliably honors every prohibition just because it is written.

followups.q: Sometimes a negative is unavoidable. How do you make a 'do not' as reliable as possible?
followups.a: Pair the prohibition with the positive alternative so there's a concrete action to take instead—'never quote a price; instead say pricing is handled by sales'—so the model isn't just maintaining an absence. Keep it singular and specific rather than buried in a list, and for anything truly hard back it with a deterministic output filter.

followups.q: Why does a long list of prohibitions dilute each rule rather than add up?
followups.a: Attention and instruction-following are finite; as the list grows the model is likelier to satisfy some rules and quietly miss others, and lengthy constraint blocks also push real content toward the weak middle of context. More rules don't compound to more compliance—they compete.

followups.q: How do you verify the model actually honors a constraint in production?
followups.a: Don't trust the wording—measure it: add adversarial and edge cases targeting the constraint to your eval set and gate prompt changes on the pass rate, then sample production traffic with an output check for violations. For hard rules, the output filter both enforces and instruments compliance.

q: When would Tree-of-Thoughts or another search-based reasoning method beat linear chain-of-thought, and why is it rarely the default?
a: Chain-of-thought commits to one linear path, so an early wrong turn dooms the answer, whereas Tree-of-Thoughts explores multiple branches, evaluates partial states, and backtracks, which helps on problems with large search spaces like planning, puzzles, or multi-step proofs. The win comes from deliberate search rather than a single greedy trajectory. It is rarely the default because it multiplies calls and cost by exploring and scoring many branches, adds orchestration complexity, and needs a usable state-evaluation signal. For most tasks plain CoT or self-consistency captures the gains far more cheaply, so reserve tree search for genuinely combinatorial problems.
fs: Frames ToT as branching search with backtracking for combinatorial problems, and explains the call-count cost that keeps it off the default path.
fj: Reaching for Tree-of-Thoughts on ordinary tasks where linear CoT or self-consistency would be far cheaper and sufficient.

followups.q: ToT needs to evaluate partial states. What happens if that evaluator is unreliable?
followups.a: The search is only as good as its state-evaluation signal: a noisy or miscalibrated evaluator prunes promising branches and pursues dead ends, so you pay the full multi-branch cost while getting worse results than plain CoT. Often the evaluator is itself an LLM call, compounding both cost and error.

followups.q: How does Tree-of-Thoughts differ from self-consistency, since both use multiple paths?
followups.a: Self-consistency samples complete independent chains and majority-votes the final answers—no intermediate evaluation or backtracking. ToT builds a search tree, scoring and pruning partial states and backtracking from dead ends before reaching an answer. Self-consistency is embarrassingly parallel and cheap to implement; ToT is sequential, stateful, and needs an evaluator.

followups.q: Have stronger native reasoning models reduced the need for explicit ToT scaffolding?
followups.a: Largely yes—models with strong internal reasoning do a lot of implicit exploration and self-correction within their own chain, so hand-built tree-search orchestration earns its keep on fewer problems than before. For most tasks, routing to a capable reasoning model with a clear goal beats bolting external search onto a weaker one.

q: How do you decide between one well-crafted prompt and a multi-step prompt chain for a given task?
a: Start with a single prompt; it is cheaper, lower-latency, and easier to cache and debug, and modern models handle a lot in one pass. Move to a chain when the task bundles distinct sub-decisions that interfere, when you need a structured intermediate artifact to lock in (a classification before drafting), or when different steps want different models, temperatures, or tools. Chaining buys isolation and debuggability per step at the cost of more calls, more latency, and orchestration and error-handling surface. The deciding question is whether splitting genuinely improves reliability or you are just adding hops, so measure end-to-end quality and cost both ways before committing.
fs: Defaults to single-prompt and chains only when sub-decisions interfere or steps need different models, weighing reliability against added latency and hops.
fj: Reflexively chaining everything into many steps, adding latency and failure surface where one good prompt would do.

followups.q: A chain reduced each step's error but the overall task got less reliable. How?
followups.a: End-to-end reliability is the product of per-step reliability, so a five-step chain where each step is 95% correct compounds to roughly 77% overall—each added hop is another multiplicative failure point, plus orchestration and parsing bugs between steps. Splitting only wins if isolation raises per-step accuracy enough to beat that compounding.

followups.q: Does chaining help or hurt prompt caching and latency?
followups.a: It usually hurts both: each step is a separate round-trip adding sequential latency, and a chain has multiple distinct prompts so there's no single long static prefix to cache as effectively as one well-structured call. A single prompt keeps one cacheable prefix and one network hop.

followups.q: What's a hybrid between one prompt and a full chain when the task is borderline?
followups.a: Keep a single call but structure internal stages within it: a schema that emits a reasoning/classification field before the answer field, so the model commits intermediate results in one pass without separate round-trips. You get some of the 'decide before drafting' benefit at single-call cost and latency.

q: How do you run an eval-driven loop to optimize a prompt, and how do you know when to stop tuning?
a: Lock a versioned eval set of real and adversarial cases with a metric (deterministic checks plus a calibrated judge), then change one variable at a time and measure the delta so you can attribute the effect rather than guessing. Keep a held-out slice the optimizer never sees to catch overfitting to your eval cases, since prompts can be tuned to game a small set. Stop when gains flatten within noise, when remaining errors are data, retrieval, or model-capability problems a prompt cannot fix, or when further tuning trades measurable cost and latency for marginal quality. Track each prompt version against its eval run so any regression is bisectable and reproducible.
fs: Runs a one-variable-at-a-time loop on a versioned eval with a held-out slice, and stops when gains hit noise or the bottleneck is no longer the prompt.
fj: Endlessly tweaking wording against the same small set, overfitting to it, with no held-out slice or stopping criterion.

followups.q: A 2-point eval gain on 80 examples looks like progress. Is it real?
followups.a: Probably not distinguishable from noise: on a small set, a couple of items flipping is a 2-point swing well within sampling variance, so you can't claim improvement without a significance check or a larger set. Treat small deltas as noise unless they replicate on a held-out slice or the set is big enough for the effect to clear the confidence band.

followups.q: Why change only one variable at a time, given how slow that makes the loop?
followups.a: Attribution: if you change wording, examples, and format together and the score moves, you can't tell which change helped or whether two effects canceled, so you learn nothing transferable and may keep a harmful change masked by a helpful one. One-variable-at-a-time is slower but gives causal signal you can carry to the next model or prompt.

followups.q: How do you recognize the bottleneck is no longer the prompt?
followups.a: When the residual errors cluster on missing or wrong retrieved context, ambiguous or mislabeled eval data, or tasks beyond the model's capability—not on phrasing the model could follow. Inspect the failures: if better wording can't fix them because the needed information isn't present or the model can't do the reasoning, stop tuning the prompt and fix retrieval, data, or model choice.
